name: NexCMS
tagline: Headless content management system built for developers
description: NexCMS is a headless CMS — a content management system where the editing interface is separated from the front end that displays the content. Developers connect their own frontend; editors manage content through a clean dashboard. No opinionated templating, no framework lock-in.
forWho: Developers who need a flexible content backend without being tied to a specific front-end framework. Teams that want editors to manage content without touching code.
story: Built as a learning project that turned into something useful. The goal was to understand how headless CMS architectures actually work by building one from scratch — content modeling, API design, role-based access, media handling. It's become a useful internal tool for some of my other projects.
details: Content types defined via JSON schema. REST + GraphQL API output. Role-based access control for editor, author, and admin roles. Media upload and management. Built to be self-hosted or deployed to any Node-compatible platform.
detailedSpecs: Content type system: each content type is defined as a JSON schema document stored in the database — fields include name, type (text, rich_text, number, boolean, date, media, relation), required flag, and validation rules. The schema is compiled at runtime into a Zod validator used for both API input validation and form generation in the editor UI. REST API: GET /api/content/:type returns paginated entries; POST /api/content/:type creates; PATCH/DELETE scoped to entry ID. All endpoints protected by JWT middleware with role checks (admin: full access; editor: create/update own; author: draft only). GraphQL layer built with graphql-yoga — types generated dynamically from content type schemas via a schema builder that walks the JSON definition. Media handled via multer upload to local storage or S3-compatible bucket (configurable via env). PostgreSQL with a generic entries table (id, type, data JSONB, created_by, created_at, updated_at) — the JSONB column stores all content fields, letting the schema evolve without migrations. Fulltext search via PostgreSQL's tsvector on the JSONB content field.
